content:
Why is the borrow_mut method designed in the way it is? It seems rather weird.

For example, lets say I call borrow_mut on an Arc<T>, then I get something that derefs to &mut Arc<T>, which means that I can swap it for another Arc<T>. Is that how its intended to work?

Also, using the scope guard like this seems like a leaky abstraction.

Also, why does the trait always provide borrow_mut? Most similar traits in std have separate traits for immutable and mutable access, meaning that a type may choose to only implement the immutable one.
Related, I have a todo to add a lifetime parameter to the scope guard returned by borrow_mut()

Alice Ryhl said:

Why is the borrow_mut method designed in the way it is? It seems rather weird.

For example, lets say I call borrow_mut on an Arc<T>, then I get something that derefs to &mut Arc<T>, which means that I can swap it for another Arc<T>. Is that how its intended to work?

Also, using the scope guard like this seems like a leaky abstraction.

It looks to me like this might have some issues in light of core::mem::replace()/swap()/take().
If you replace the Arc<T> that is behind the &mut you can obtain through borrow_mut(), the Arc that was there will be dropped. Refcount might go to zero and leave a dangling pointer in C land.

Further, when ScopeGuard guarding the &mut is dropped, it will into_foreign() the new Arc<T> and it will leak.

This is not the intention as far as I know. I don't think we considered this.
Perhaps we could check if the address of the pointee changed when the borrow_mut() is over and then update the pointer
Anyhow, it should be possible to bound the lifetime of the ScopeGuard returned by borrow_mut()
IMO it should not be a scope guard.

Alice Ryhl said:

IMO it should not be a scope guard.

What instead?
I would do the same as the immutable guard.
Not following. The immutable borrow is not using a guard?
By "immutable guard" I am referring to the Borrowed associated type in the ForeignOwnable trait.
My suggestion is to add a BorrowedMut associated type and return that.
Are there even any users of borrow_mut?

Alice Ryhl said:

Are there even any users of borrow_mut?

I had a use in the radix tree, but since that is deprecated that is not going anywhere

/// Search for `key` in the map. Return a mutable reference to the
    /// associated value if found.
    pub fn get_mut(&mut self, key: Key) -> Option<MutBorrow<'_, V>> {
        let item =
            core::ptr::NonNull::new(unsafe { bindings::radix_tree_lookup(self.tree.get(), key) })?;

        // SAFETY: `item` was created by a call to
        // `ForeignOwnable::into_foreign()`. As `get()` takes a `&self` and
        // `remove()` takes a `&mut self`, no borrows for `item` can exist and
        // `ForeignOwnable::from_foreign()` cannot be called until this borrow
        // is dropped.
        Some(MutBorrow {
            guard: unsafe { V::borrow_mut(item.as_ptr()) },
            _marker: core::marker::PhantomData,
        })
    }

Alice Ryhl said:

My suggestion is to add a BorrowedMut associated type and return that.

That is fine but does not really solve anything. We would have to put a bound on the lifetime and update the safety comment to specify that caller must call into_foreign() when done with the reference. I guess that if it is the responsibility of the caller of borrow_mut() to make sure that into_foreign() is called, we can make it the responsibility of that caller to update the C pointer if the target of the smart pointer has changed.
I'm not convinced that an Arc should have a borrow_mut in the first place.
For a box, it could deref to &mut T so that you can't swap the box.
Basically compare the result of into_foreign() when the original c_void passed to borrow_mut() and if they differ, update the place in C land where the pointer came from in the first place

Alice Ryhl said:

I'm not convinced that an Arc should have a borrow_mut in the first place.

You are right, it makes sense to split the trait.

Alice Ryhl said:

Why is the borrow_mut method designed in the way it is? It seems rather weird.

For example, lets say I call borrow_mut on an Arc<T>, then I get something that derefs to &mut Arc<T>, which means that I can swap it for another Arc<T>. Is that how its intended to work?

Also, using the scope guard like this seems like a leaky abstraction.

I agree, this seems wrong. Also agree with the approach to have a mut trait with this functionality that we implement just for box.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Basically compare the result of into_foreign() when the original c_void passed to borrow_mut() and if they differ, update the place in C land where the pointer came from in the first place

how do you know the location in C land? what if there are multiple refs? I dont think this is possible

y86 said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Basically compare the result of into_foreign() when the original c_void passed to borrow_mut() and if they differ, update the place in C land where the pointer came from in the first place

how do you know the location in C land? what if there are multiple refs? I dont think this is possible

Yes, you are right. One would have to always pass in a ** and we would not know if it is the only one.
If borrow_mut() returns a &mut to the target of the smart pointer, we should be fine. The object might be swapped out, but at least the allocation will contain a valid object, right?

y86 said:

Alice Ryhl said:

Why is the borrow_mut method designed in the way it is? It seems rather weird.

For example, lets say I call borrow_mut on an Arc<T>, then I get something that derefs to &mut Arc<T>, which means that I can swap it for another Arc<T>. Is that how its intended to work?

Also, using the scope guard like this seems like a leaky abstraction.

I agree, this seems wrong. Also agree with the approach to have a mut trait with this functionality that we implement just for box.

Maybe "borrow" is the wrong part of the name.

If I remember correctly, the usage is during a series of callbacks, the foreign owned data needs to be accessed multiple times (so manually from_foreign and into_foreign is a bit annoying), and some of the callback implementation may modify the underlying data, so a deref to &mut ForeignOwnable is returned, of course that only works if Box<..> is used, but the different implementations may use either Arc or Box, and having a borrow_mut makes Box case and Arc case share the generic callback handling code.

For example: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust/rust/kernel/fs.rs#L139

A had some interesting thoughts regarding borrow_mut and xarray. See:

This probably depends on changes to borrow_mut. I think ideally we want the trait to have a BorrowedMut type that represents the kind of access you can get to the inner value given mutable access to the T, then return Option<T::BorrowedMut<'a>>.

For an Arc, the BorrowedMut type would still be ArcBorrow, but that actually still gives this extra powers over the two other methods. It lets you access the Arcs contents without modifying the refcount, and without a closure.

And of course, with a Box, the BorrowedMut type would be &mut T, so this type would return Option<&mut T> when the pointer type is Box.

<https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/1019#discussion_r1254112511>

I'll resend this patch with the updated comment here: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20230822093148.3478340-1-aliceryhl@google.com/

Any objections?
